coredata.dc:description: Disturbed intestinal microbiota and abnormally high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the colon are important factors in the pathogenesis of ulcerative colitis (UC). Therefore, modulation of intestinal microbiota and ROS synchronously in the inflamed colon are effective approach for UC management. In this study, we developed carbon dots (C-dots) nanozymes derived from glutathione and biotin via a solvothermal method. We found that the synthesized C-dots nanozymes possessed both the enzymatic activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and the ability to regulate intestinal microbiota. The in vitro experiments showed that C-dots nanozymes could eliminate excess ROS, protect cells from oxidative stress, and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and interleukin-1β (IL-1β). In the mice models of UC, oral administration of C-dots nanozymes in chitosan/alginate hydrogel was found effective in alleviating colonic inflammation such as endoscopic and pathological remission with preventive and therapeutic effects. In addition, C-dots showed a remarkable ability to modulate the composition of the gut microbiota as well as increased potential probiotics and decreased pathogenic microorganisms. Moreover, with satisfactory red fluorescence, C-dots showed feasible bioimaging for intestinal inflammation. Overall, C-dots nanozymes achieved desirable preventive and therapeutic effects for UC by scavenging ROS and modulating the intestinal microbiota, coupled with bioimaging properties, C-dots nanozymes provide a potential approach for the clinical treatment of UC patients.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of C-dots nanozymes and their application in UC therapy. (A) Synthesis of the C-dots nanozymes and their properties for ROS scavenging and bioimaging. (B) The applications of the C-dots nanozymes in the treatment of UC. C-dots nanozymes were loaded in chitosan/alginate hydrogel for oral delivery to the inflamed colon. At the intestinal inflammatory site, the hydrogel was disrupted and the released C-dots nanozymes exhibited preventive and therapeutic effects on UC by regulating ROS levels and intestinal microbiota.

Characterization and enzymatic activities of C-dots nanozymes. (A) TEM of the C-dots. Scale bar: 20 nm. (B) HR-TEM images and lattice of the C-dots. Scale bar: 5 nm. (C) Statistical plot of C-dots particle size. (D) FT-IR result of C-dots. (E) Zeta potential of C-dots. (F) The fluorescence emission spectrum of C-dots at different pH conditions. (G) Total antioxidant capacity of C-dots at different time points. (H) SOD enzyme activity of C-dots (WST-1). (I) The •OH removal capacity of the C-dots.

Anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of C-dots nanozymes. (A) Co-localization of C-dots with mitochondria. Scale bar: 20 μm. (B) Fluorescence images of RAW 264.7 cells after incubation with DCFH probe under different conditions. Scale bar: 100 μm. (C) mRNA expression levels of cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 in RAW 264.7 cells under different treatment conditions. (D) Flow cytometry results of apoptosis under different conditions. (E) AM/PI staining images of RAW 264.7 cells under H2O2 treatment. Scale bar: 100 μm.

In vivo intestine bioimaging of C-dots nanozymes. (A) In vivo imaging of healthy mice at different time points after gavage of 2.5 mg/kg C-dots. (B) In vivo imaging of healthy mice at different time points injected with 2.5 mg/kg C-dots in the tail vein. (C) Fluorescence intensity statistics for the isolated colon in Fig. A, B (n = 3). (D) Comparison of the distribution in the digestive tracts between DSS-induced colitis mice and healthy mice (E) Fluorescence intensity statistics for the colon in Fig. D (n = 3).

Preventive effect of C-dots nanozymes in ulcerative colitis. (A) Schematic of C-dots treatment (By Figdraw). (B) Perianal blood in stool in mice. Yellow circles are marked for mice with blood in the stool. (C) Endoscopic images of the colon in different groups of mice. Yellow arrows mark the lesion sites. (D) Images of the colon. (E) H&E staining results of different groups. Scale bar: 200 μm.Yellow arrows mark the lesion sites. (F) Rate of body weight change in mice. (G) DAI scores in mice. (H) Intestinal MPO activity index. (I) Mouse spleen weights. (J) mRNA expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β).

Therapeutic effect of C-dots nanozymes in ulcerative colitis. (A) Schematic of C-dots treatment (By Figdraw). (B) Rate of body weight changes. (C) The spleen graphs. (D) The weight of the spleen. (E) Endoscopic images of different groups of mice before and after treatment. Yellow arrows mark the lesion sites. (F) H&E staining results in different groups. Scale bar: 200 μm. Yellow arrows mark the lesion sites.(G) mRNA expression of different pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-12).

Effect of C-dots on intestinal microbiota. (A) Grouping and dominant species (family level) for each sample. (B) Heat map of species composition at the genus level. (C) Chao1 Index. (D) ACE Index. (E) Principal component analysis of species composition at the Genus level. (F) Heat map of differential species clustering at the family level. (G) Differences in microbial taxa were analyzed by linear discriminant analysis of effect size (LEfSe). (H) Species evolutionary branching diagram.

Biocompatibility evaluation of C-dots. (A) RAW 264.7 cell viability after co-incubation with different concentrations of C-dots. (B) Body weight changes of mice. (C) Blood biochemical parameters in mice (The yellow dotted line shows the range of normal values). (D) The flow-cytometry results of apoptosis of RAW 264.7 cells after incubation with different concentrations of C-dots. (E) H&E staining of major organs. Scale bar: 100 μm. (F) H&E staining of the whole GI tract. Scale bar: 500 μm.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Disturbed intestinal microbiota and abnormally high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the colon are important factors in the pathogenesis of ulcerative colitis (UC). Therefore, modulation of intestinal microbiota and ROS synchronously in the inflamed colon are effective approach for UC management. In this study, we developed carbon dots (C-dots) nanozymes derived from glutathione and biotin via a solvothermal method. We found that the synthesized C-dots nanozymes possessed both the enzymatic activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and the ability to regulate intestinal microbiota. The in vitro experiments showed that C-dots nanozymes could eliminate excess ROS, protect cells from oxidative stress, and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and interleukin-1β (IL-1β). In the mice models of UC, oral administration of C-dots nanozymes in chitosan/alginate hydrogel was found effective in alleviating colonic inflammation such as endoscopic and pathological remission with preventive and therapeutic effects. In addition, C-dots showed a remarkable ability to modulate the composition of the gut microbiota as well as increased potential probiotics and decreased pathogenic microorganisms. Moreover, with satisfactory red fluorescence, C-dots showed feasible bioimaging for intestinal inflammation. Overall, C-dots nanozymes achieved desirable preventive and therapeutic effects for UC by scavenging ROS and modulating the intestinal microbiota, coupled with bioimaging properties, C-dots nanozymes provide a potential approach for the clinical treatment of UC patients.

ce:keyword.ce:text: linear discriminant analysis of effect size

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article and its Supplementary Information files. The datasets used or analyzed during the study are available upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic nonspecific inflammatory disease of the intestinal tract, along with Crohn's disease (CD), belongs to inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) [1]. UC is a superficial inflammation that primarily involves the colonic mucosa, whereas CD is usually a patchy, transmural inflammation that can involve anywhere from the mouth to the anus [2,3]. Patients with UC often suffer from persistent or recurrent diarrhea, mucopurulent bloody stools with abdominal pain, urgency, and varying degrees of systemic symptoms, which severely affect their quality of life.

The medical treatments for IBD mainly include aminosalicylic acid [4,5], immunosuppressants [6], targeted drugs [7], and fecal transplantation. However, all of them have distinct shortcomings. For example, aminosalicylic acid analogs are indicated for patients with mild to moderate IBD, however, they are not effective in patients with severe disease. Multiple retrospective clinical meta-analyses have shown that 5-amino salicylic acid (5-ASA) exhibited adverse events including gastrointestinal symptoms, headache, and thus worsening ulcerative colitis [4,5]. Glucocorticoids and immunosuppressive agents are also the drug choice for UC, but their adverse effects were also evident [8–10]. Infliximab monoclonal antibody is currently the brightest biological agent for acute severe UC. However, about 1/3 of patients exhibit no response, and 1/3 develop a secondary loss of response, which subsequently requires a dose increase or medication change [11]. Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) refers to the transplantation of bacteria from healthy human feces into the patient's intestinal tract to re-establish normal intestinal flora, which also revealed low clinical remission rates [12–14]. Therefore, exploiting novel therapies is important for the management of UC.

Although the pathogenesis of IBD is still unclear, ROS and intestinal microbiota have been reported to be related to the pathogenesis of IBD [15–17]. Disturbances in the gut microbiota caused by environmental and/or host factors, leading to dysregulated host-microbe interactions, are important factors in accelerating disease progression in IBD [15,16]. Clinical studies have reported that similar gut flora phenotypes existed among IBD patients, such as the decrease in microbial diversity and relative abundance [18–22]. In addition, during the progression of IBD, ROS was overproduced, which then disrupted the intestinal immune system and exacerbated IBD by damaging lipids, proteins, and DNA by targeting the cysteine redox switch [23–26]. Although the development of IBD may be the result of a combination of factors, excessive ROS and disturbances in the intestinal flora are predisposing factors. Studies have confirmed that ROS are involved in intestinal stem cell (ISC) maintenance and IEC defense against intracellular bacteria [27,28]. Similarly, gut flora may influence ROS production [29,30]. Therefore, supplementation with antioxidants to scavenge excessive ROS while regulating the intestinal flora may become an important tool to alleviate IBD.

Nanozymes are nanoparticles with enzyme-like activity, which have the advantages of mimicking the activity of natural enzymes, high stability, low cost, and easy modification, which have received increasing attention in biomedical applications [31,32]. Our previous study has demonstrated that C-dots owned anti-oxidant enzymatic activity and C-dots were effective in treating UC and CD [33]. However, C-dots via intravenous administration were quickly excreted via the kidney with a half-life of only 11.5 min, which may affect the duration of their therapeutic effects. Secondly, oral administration is safer compared with tail vein injection, which is an invasive procedure. Oral administration is the most widely used route during drug treatment [34]. Compared to intravenous administration, oral administration exhibits more significant advantages in improving patient compliance, avoiding side effects, and improving the life quality of patients. Therefore, the oral route is the first choice for UC patients who need long-term drug control due to the recurrence. Moreover, the small size and stable nature of C-dots make them more suitable for oral delivery methods as small molecular drugs are more able to avoid the multiple heterogeneous biological barriers of the gastrointestinal tract [35].

In this study, we constructed C-dots from glutathione (L-glutathione, GSH) and biotin by the solvothermal method, and the obtained C-dots had small particle size, stable fluorescence properties, and high enzymatic activity. In vitro LPS-induced cellular model, the C-dots showed excellent anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant effects. Then in the UC mice models, C-dots were delivered to the colon through chitosan/alginate hydrogel by oral administration, which was shown to exert a favorable anti-inflammatory effect in vivo. The C-dots nanozymes could mitigate colonic inflammation by scavenging ROS and regulating the intestinal microbiota, reducing the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, and the results were superior to those of the clinical first-line drugs, 5-ASA. Therefore, oral administration of C-dots nanozymes will provide a new idea for the multi-pathway treatment of UC (Fig. 1 ).

D-Biotin was purchased from Sangong Biotechnology Co. Glutathione (GSH), formamide, and sodium alginate were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co Ltd (Shanghai, China). Chitosan was purchased from Tokyo Kasei Kogyo Co Ltd (Tokyo, Japan). Mito-Tracker Green (mitochondrial green fluorescent probe), Annexin V-FITC Apoptosis Detection Kit, and Reactive Oxygen Detection Kit were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology. Dextran Sodium Sulfate (DSS) and Fecal Genomic DNA Extraction Kit (magnetic bead method) were obtained from MP Biomedicals (California, USA). All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm, microporous). All chemicals are of analytical grade and are ready to use without further purification.

To synthesize C-dots, 30 mL of formamide, 0.9 g of D-biotin, and 0.9 g of GSH were added together, stirred well, and then the suspension was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was dialyzed in deionized water (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) 3500 Da) for 7 d. The product was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter, then concentrated and dialyzed again, and finally concentrated and lyophilized.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images are obtained from a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Nicolet 5700 instrument. Zeta potential was measured by a Malvern particle sizer (Nano ZSE). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Hitachi spectrophotometer (HITACHI-F4700). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using an ultraviolet absorption spectrophotometer (UV-2700).

RAW 264.7 cells were cultured in 75 cm2 Petri dishes at 37 °C in a humid atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. RAW 264.7 was cultured in a cell-specific medium (Procell, Wuhan, China). In LPS-induced macrophage inflammation, RAW 264.7 cells were stimulated with 100 ng/mL LPS for 8 h.

Male C57BL/6 mice (6–8 w, 20–25 g) were purchased from Jin Pharmaceutical Science and Technology Co Ltd, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, China. All mice were kept under standard conditions, including light, temperature, water, and food, and animal experiments were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Xi'an Jiaotong University.

We constructed an acute colitis model. 30 male C57BL/6 mice were divided into 5 groups. The control group was given normal drinking water. Model group mice were continuously drinking water containing 2.5 % DSS for 7d to construct colitis. An endoscopy was performed after the 8 d to assess the modeling. After successful modeling, three consecutive doses were administered, and at the end of the administration, the treatment was again endoscopically assessed, and the evaluation of the endoscopic scores followed the Mayo Score criteria (Table S1) [36]. Their feces, weight, and other status were observed and recorded daily. On day 12, after the execution of all mice, the colon was collected for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, and qPCR assay.

Twenty-four male C57BL/6 mice were divided into four groups. No treatment was given to the control group. Model mice were continued drinking water containing 2.5 % DSS to construct colitis mice for 7 days. The drug was administered every other day three times starting from the second day. The feces, weight, and other status of mice were observed and recorded daily. Before execution, the colon was viewed through an endoscope. On day 8, the colon was collected for H&E staining and qPCR after the execution of all mice.

The feces of each group of mice were collected separately into enzyme-free test tubes. Subsequently, the DNA of the fecal flora was extracted using a fecal genomic DNA extraction kit (magnetic bead method), and the 16S amplicon analysis and sequencing of the intestinal flora were carried out by junshan biopharmaceutical co. Sample processing includes genomic DNA extraction, PCR amplification, and product purification, PCR product quantification and homogenization, sequencing library construction, and double-end sequencing. The raw image data files obtained from high-throughput sequencing (such as Illumina HiSeq and other sequencing platforms) are analyzed by Base Calling and converted into raw sequenced sequences (Sequenced Reads).

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-tests in GraphPad Prism 8 was used to determine statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001); p-values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

The TEM image showed that the obtained C-dots were uniformly distributed and monodisperse (Fig. 2 A). The C-dots had clear lattice stripes with an average d-spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2B), which corresponded to the (100) face of graphite [37]. The average size of the C-dots was 2.4 ± 0.4 nm according to the statistical result (Fig. 2C). The structure and composition of the C-dots were further analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements. A broad absorption band between 3000 and 3700 cm−1 was measured, which belonged to the OH/NH stretching vibration. Besides, the peaks at 1380 cm−1, 1690 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, and 1158 cm−1 belonged to CN, CO, CC/CN, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2D). The FT-IR results showed the presence of abundant functional groups in the C-dots. In addition, the zeta potential of C-dots was ∼ -28.3 mV (Fig. 2E), indicating that C-dots have better colloidal dispersion and stability in aqueous solution. The prepared C-dots exhibited absorption in the wavelength range from 350-450 nm, and 550–750 nm due to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of the aromatic π system containing the CO, CN, and CS bonds of Fig. S1A. We further evaluated the effect of pH on the fluorescence performance of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots diminished at pH 3.5 (simulated gastric acid), which remained nearly unchanged at simulated intestinal pH 5.5–7.5, indicating that C-dots could be used for bioimaging at the abovementioned pH. Further, we also investigated the change in fluorescence intensity of C-dots under different excitation lights (Fig. S1B).

Following, the antioxidant properties of the C-dots were evaluated. We first assessed the total antioxidant capacity by detecting the absorbance at 734 nm after co-incubation of different concentrations of C-dots and ABTS reagents. As shown in Fig. 2G, the inhibition rate of ABTS+ increased with increasing concentration of C-dots and incubation time. We then evaluated the SOD enzymatic activity and •OH scavenging capacity of C-dots by the nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) method, SOD Assay Kit (WST), ESR assay and methylene blue (MB) colorimetric method, respectively. NBT acts as a probe for O2 •− could be reduced to blue methylhydrazine with a maximum absorption peak at 560 nm, which could be inhibited by C-dots. When the concentration of C-dots was increased to 100 μg/mL, the inhibition rate reached 80 % (Fig. S1C). For the superoxide dismutase assay kit with WST, superoxide anions, the substrate of SOD, could bind and reduce the highly water-soluble WST; and the ratio of the reduced WST is linearly related to xanthine oxidase activity which could be inhibited by SOD. Through calculating, the SOD-like enzymatic unit of C-dots could be obtained. The result showed that the SOD activity of C-dots increased with the increasing concentrations of C-dots which is in accordance with the results of NBT (Fig. S1C), the enzymatic unit was 3299 U/mg (Fig. 2H). In addition, we evaluated the SOD-like activity of C-dots by ESR assay. During the ESR assay, the spin adducts formed after the superoxide anions are trapped by the free radical catcher, DMPO, whose signal would be changed after adding the SOD-like materials. Therefore, by recording the ESR signal intensity, the superoxide anions scavenging capability of C-dots could be evaluated. The results showed that a significant decrease in the ESR signal of DMPO/•OOH was observed when 100 μg/mL of C-dot nanozymes was added (Fig. S1D), which suggested that the C-dot nanozymes exhibited SOD-like activity. The •OH produced by Fenton's reagent oxidizes and discolors methylene blue (MB), so MB can be used as an •OH indicator. As shown in Fig. 2I, MB alone showed a blue color with maximum absorption at 660 nm, when Fenton's reagent was added, the color of MB faded and there was almost no absorption at 660 nm, and the blue color was restored after the addition of C-dots. These results suggested that C-dots could scavenge ROS such as O2 •− as well as •OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of C-dots nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular mitochondria are the main organelles that generate ROS and induce oxidative damage. Considering the relationship between mitochondria and ROS production, we first assessed the co-localization of C-dots with mitochondria. The red fluorescence of C-dots was able to overlap with the green fluorescence of the mitochondria probe, indicating that C-dots were able to enter the mitochondria of RAW 264.7 cells (Fig. 3 A). Further statistical results also confirmed that C-dots were able to co-localize with mitochondria, with Pearson’s correlation coefficient (PCC) and Manders' coefficients of 0.902, 0.9651 (M1), and 0.7487 (M2), respectively (Fig. S2A and B). When bacterial-produced lipopolysaccharide (LPS) invades the organism, macrophages mediate an immune response by way of mitochondrial production of ROS [38,39]. However, excess ROS can cause oxidative damage to normal cells and aggravate inflammation. Thus, the removal of excess ROS would attenuate the cellular inflammatory response. Based on the antioxidant properties of the C-dots, we investigated the effect of C-dots on ROS clearance in the LPS-induced macrophage RAW 264.7 inflammation model. 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) is a green fluorescent probe for ROS. When ROS are present in the cell, DCFH-DA is oxidized to 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) which emits green fluorescence. RAW 264.7 cells were first incubated with C-dots for 8 h, then the cells were treated with 100 ng/mL LPS for 8 h. Intracellular ROS levels were measured by DCFH-DA. As shown in Fig. 3B and S2C, the control group had almost no fluorescent signal. The fluorescence intensity of the cells in the LPS-treated group was significantly enhanced, which was significantly weakened after incubation with C-dots, indicating that C-dots could scavenge ROS.

Excess ROS promotes an inflammatory cascade waterfall response through oxidative stress, with overexpression of the pro-inflammatory factors including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, further exacerbating tissue damage. Therefore, we further evaluated the effects of C-dots on the mRNA expression of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. The results (Fig. 3C) showed that after LPS induction TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β were elevated about 10-fold, 300-fold, and 20-fold, respectively, whereas they were significantly reduced when C-dots were pre-treated, suggesting that C-dots had a good anti-inflammatory ability.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is one of the main forms of ROS that causes oxidative stress and then induces apoptosis. We therefore investigated the role of C-dots on apoptosis through Annexin V/PI in a model of H2O2-induced oxidative stress in cells. In the early stage of apoptosis, phosphatidylserine (PS) can be flipped from the inside of the cell membrane to the surface of the cell membrane. Annexin V is a Ca2+-dependent phospholipid-binding protein that binds specifically to PS with high affinity. PI, as a nuclear staining dye, does not cross the cell membrane of living cells and therefore can only stain late apoptotic cells and dead cells. As shown in Fig. 3D, there was no significant difference in the apoptosis rate of cells between the control group and the C-dots pretreatment group alone. The apoptosis rate was increased to 22.5 % after treatment with 1 mM H2O2, whereas it was reduced to 3.49 % after pretreatment with C-dots. Similarly, C-dots could restore the decrease in cell viability induced by H2O2 (Fig. S3A). Calcein-AM is a cellular staining reagent that fluorescently labels living cells. When it enters the cytoplasm, esterase enzymes hydrolyze it into Calcein which remains in the cell and emits strong green fluorescence. PI, as a nuclear staining dye, does not cross the cell membrane of living cells and therefore can only stain dead cells. We then evaluated the ability of C-dots to protect cells from oxidative damage using AM/PI double staining. The results showed that the red fluorescence of the dead cells was significantly enhanced after treatment with 1 mM H2O2, while it gradually became weaker after treatment with C-dots (Fig. 3E and S3B). Taken together, these results suggested that C-dots exhibited preferable antioxidant and anti-inflammatory capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo intestine bioimaging of C-dots nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the fluorescence emission peak of C-dots nanozymes is located in the red region, we speculated that C-dots could be used for bioimaging in vivo. To compare the colonic accumulation of C-dots after two modes of administration, healthy mice were given 2.5 mg/kg C-dots through oral and tail veins, respectively. The abdominal fluorescence was first examined at different time points, the fluorescence intensity was gradually reduced from 1 h after tail vein injection, and it almost lost at 8 h. However, the fluorescence was last for 8 h in the oral administration group, and the fluorescence is bright even at 8 h (Fig. 4 A and B). To further compare the retention of C-dots in the colon, the whole digestive tract from the stomach to the rectum was imaged after execution, and the fluorescence intensity at the colon was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4C, the intensity of intestinal fluorescence was stronger in mice after oral C-dots at 1 h than that of tail-vein injection, and this state was maintained for 8 h. This suggested that oral C-dots were retained in the colon for a longer time than the tail-vein injection, indicating oral C-dots might accumulate a longer time at the colonic site. Due to the differences in pH and electrical nuclei-carrying proteins in the colons of ulcerative colitis mice and normal mice, we further compared the distribution differences in the digestive tracts between DSS-induced colitis mice and healthy mice (Fig. 4D and E). We found that there was stronger fluorescence intensity in colitis mice than that in healthy mice. Therefore, oral administration of C-dots might exert a better therapeutic effect on colonic inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preventive effect of C-dots nanozymes on ulcerative colitis
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the desirable anti-inflammation, anti-oxidation, and satisfactory accumulation in the colon of C-dots, we further validated the effect of C-dots on UC disease progression. Mice were divided into 4 groups: control, DSS modeling, 5-ASA, and C-dots nanozymes (Fig. 5 A). The treatment group received oral C-dots 2.5 mg/kg per day three times. The mice were then killed on day 8. The weight of mice began to lose on day 5, blood in the stool was visible in the anus, and DAI scores were elevated, indicating the onset of inflammation (Fig. 5B). Endoscopic is an important tool in the diagnosis, efficacy assessment, follow-up and treatment of UC. As shown in Fig. 5C, blurred colonic vessels and mucosal bleeding with secretion of pus were seen in the DSS-treated group, which was significantly removed after C-dots treatment. Meanwhile, DSS induced a decrease in colon length, whereas after C-dots treatment the colon length was increased (Fig. 5D). Further microscopic evaluation by H&E staining showed that the epithelium was disrupted, crypts disappeared, and inflammatory cells were infiltrated (Fig. 5E). In contrast, C-dots administration made the restoration of colon structure and reduction of immune infiltration. We also found the same results for the rate of change in body weight, disease activity index (DAI) scores, and tissue MPO scores in mice, with significantly lower scores after C-dots treatment (Fig. 5F–H). Similarly, mice in the C-dots treatment group had significantly reduced spleen weight (Fig. 5I). Next, we examined the expression levels of cytokines and the results showed that cytokines including TNF-α and IL-1β were increased in the DSS group, and their expression was significantly reduced in the C-dots group (Fig. 5J and K).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The therapeutic effect of C-dots nanozymes on ulcerative colitis
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic role of C-dots nanozymes was further investigated (Fig. 6 A). After three consecutive gavage treatments and another endoscopy, the mice were executed to collect the spleen and colon. The weight loss lasted to day 12 in the DSS group, while it was reduced in the C-dots group, which was superior to that of the clinical first-line therapeutic drug 5-ASA (Fig. 6B). The spleen is an important immune organ, and the size and weight of the spleen increased significantly in the DSS group due to inflammatory infiltration. In contrast, they were significantly decreased after C-dots treatment (Fig. 6C and D). As for the lesions of the colon, ulcers of different sizes, white pus moss, fecal irregularity, mucosal hemorrhage, and blurred vascular texture were observed in different groups before treatment. After treatment, the phenomena disappeared to different degrees, and oral C-dots were more effective than oral 5-ASA and intravenous C-dots (Fig. 6E). The mayo endoscopic scoring of the endoscopic images also affirmed the therapeutic effect of C-dots (Fig. S4). We then performed H&E staining of the colons to analyze the structural and pathological changes. As shown in Fig. 6F, the DSS-treated mice showed crypt loss, cup cell destruction, and inflammatory cell infiltration compared to healthy mice, which were remarkably alleviated by oral C-dots. To assess the mitigating effects on inflammation, we further examined the expression of cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12 in the colon. As shown in Fig. 6G, the levels of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12 were significantly increased after DSS induction, which was significantly decreased after C-dots treatment. In general, oral C-dots exhibited better therapeutic effects on inflammation resolution than 5-ASA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dots on intestinal microbiota
ce:section.ce:para: The gut microbiota is one of the densest microbial communities in nature, and the microorganisms living in our intestines are an additional metabolic organ that primarily benefits our physiological functions, including nutrient absorption, metabolism, disease resistance, and susceptibility. The composition of the gut microbiota is unique and relatively stable [40,41]. Various exogenous and endogenous factors, such as dietary changes, infections, antibiotic use, or abdominal surgery, can lead to an imbalance of composition (ecological imbalance) [42,43], thus resulting in ulcerative colitis [15]. Therefore, to further explain the potential mechanism of C-dots nanozymes in ulcerative colitis, we analyzed the effects of C-dots on gut microbes using 16S rDNA sequencing.

The fecal samples from mice in the blank control group (n = 6), the DSS model group (n = 6), and the orally C-dots-treated group (n = 6) were collected for 16S rRNA gene sequencing. First, we analyzed the relative abundance of gut microbiota at the genus level. In the top 20 dominant species of the intestinal flora, Lachnospiraceae, Verrucomicrobiaceae, Bacteroidales_S24-7_group, Ruminococcaceae, and Bacteroidaceae constituted the major populations (Fig. 7 A). The abundance of Lachnospiraceae, Bacteroidales_S24-7_group was down-regulated in the DSS group, whereas Verrucomicrobiaceae was up-regulated, as compared to the blank control group. Whereas they were reversed after treatment with the C-dots nanzymes. Further, we analyzed heat maps of the abundance of highly abundant species in each sample at the genus level. The community heat map also revealed a significant change in the distribution of gut microbiota in DSS-treated mice, whereas there was a significant concordance in the distribution of gut microbiota in C-dots-treated mice and control mice (Fig. 7B). Bacterial expression of Lachnospiraceae_UCG-001, Roseburia, Alloprevotella and Rikenella was down-regulated and Toricibacter was up-regulated in the DSS group compared to the blank control group. After C-dots nanozymes treatment, the expression trend of these colonies was reversed. ACE, Chao1 index, and PCA (Principal Component Analysis) are the main indicators of α-diversity and β-diversity of the gut microbiota. The index (both ACE and Chao1) of the oral C-dots group was significantly higher than that of the DSS group (P < 0.05), and there was no significant difference compared with the blank control group (P > 0.05) (Fig. 7C and D). According to PCA, the flora of C-dots group and blank control group were more similar and distributed closer to each other, whereas the flora distribution of DSS group was more different (Fig. 7E). Differential species at the family (Family) level were analyzed as shown in Fig. 7F, where Bacteroidaceae, Verrucomicrobiaceae, Enterobacteriaceae, Rhodospirillaceae, and Rikenellaceae bacteria were significantly positively correlated with the DSS-induced model, and negatively correlated with the blank group and the C-dots treatment group. These results suggested that C-dots could partially reverse DSS-induced intestinal ecological dysregulation in colitis mice.

Next, we used linear discriminant analysis of effect size (LEfSe) and linear regression analysis (LDA) to determine specific enrichment in the DSS-treated and C-dots-treated groups. The results demonstrated that DSS-treated mice were found to be significantly enriched for Bacteroidaceae, Bacteroides, Turicibacter, and Rikenellaceae_RC9_gut_group, whereas C-dots-treated mice were enriched for Prevotellaceae and Alloprevotella (Fig. 7G and H). Toricibacter abundance and pathogenicity have been reported to be positively correlated with IBD condition [44], while Alloprevotella species have been reported to have a potentially beneficial effect on IBD [45]. Taken together, these results revealed that C-dots could inhibit the progression of UC by restoring the homeostasis of the gut microbiota.

The biosafety of a drug is the primary factor limiting its application to the clinic. The results of the cellular MTT assay showed that the cell viability of RAW 264.7 cells was not significantly changed at a concentration of 20 µg/mL, and flow cytometry result showed that C-dots did not lead to apoptosis (Fig. 8 A and D). In vivo, biocompatibility experiments were then performed in healthy mice. Healthy mice were given PBS and 2.5 mg/kg C-dots through oral gavage, respectively. After short-term (7 days) and long-term (30 days) administration of the C-dots, mice were collected for routine blood tests, blood biochemical analysis, and H&E staining. As shown in Fig. 8B, there was no significant difference in body weight between the control and the C-dots group. In addition, routine blood test results including red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and hemoglobin (HGB) were normal (Fig. S5). There were also no statistically significant differences in serum biochemical indicators such as liver function: alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and albumin(ALB), kidney function: blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CREA) (Fig. 8C). In the H&E staining results, no structural and pathologic changes were observed in C-dots-treated mice from the stomach to the rectum as well as in the major organs (Fig. 8E and F). The above results indicated that C-dots had good biocompatibility.

UC is a disease of chronic inflammation of the rectum and colon of uncertain etiology, which is characterized by abdominal pain, diarrhea, mucus, pus, and blood in the stools [2,46,47]. Gut microbial populations and functional diversity in patients with UC are compromised, as evidenced by a decrease in Firmicutes bacteria and an increase in Bacteroidetes bacteria and facultative anaerobes [48]. Microbiota imbalances that inhibit competition from potential pathogens promote the progression of intestinal inflammation, which is also exacerbated by excessive ROS. Restoration of gut flora homeostasis and removal of overproduced ROS may be the key to the treatment of UC.

Nanozymes are nanoparticles with high activity, simple preparation, and good stability [49]. Numerous studies have demonstrated nanozymes with anti-oxidant properties such as SOD and CAT enzymatic activities exhibited great potential in alleviating intestinal inflammation [50,51]. In this study, C-dots synthesized from GSH, biotin, and formamide exerted preferable anti-inflammatory and antioxidant capacity by scavenging ROS including O2 •− and •OH. In addition, we evaluated the changes in the colon contents in mice with different treatments through 16S sequencing. The results found that C-dots also significantly modulated the composition of the gut microbiota. First, there was a significant increase in both α-diversity and β-diversity of the gut microbiota in the oral C-dots treatment group compared to the DSS model mice. Secondly, C-dots increased potential probiotics (Lachnospiraceae_UCG-001, Roseburia, Alloprevotella, and Rikenella) and decreased pathogenic microorganisms (Turicibacter). For example, as a butyric acid-producing intestinal probiotic, Roseburia is important for restoring the intestinal barrier, thus favorable to the efficacy in inflammation resolution [52]. Therefore, through remodeling the intestinal redox environment and microecology by scavenging ROS and regulating intestinal flora simultaneously, C-dots showed good therapeutic results in the prevention and treatment of ulcerative colitis. However, since various redox signalings such as KEAP1/NRF2-ARE, and inflammatory signaling such as the NF-ĸB pathway are involved in the IBD, the biological mechanism of C-dots was not involved in this study, which needs further research.

Considering the fluorescent properties of C-dots, we compared the colonic distribution of C-dots through different administration including gavage and injection by veil. The C-dots showed more and longer time accumulation after oral administration. Furthermore, compared with healthy mice, the distribution of C-dots in the colon was significantly higher in colitis mice, which may be because chitosan-sodium alginate gel-loaded C-dots with negative charges more easily absorbed on the positive surface at the inflammatory site. It has been shown that when inflammation occurs in the intestinal mucosa, positively charged proteins (such as transferrin and inflammation-inducing antimicrobial peptides) accumulate at the site of inflammation, thereby allowing negatively charged substances to accumulate in the inflamed area of mucosal defects through electrostatic interaction [53]. For the mechanism of the C-dots, few studies identify the photoluminescence of C-dots, and carbogenic core state emission (generally showing excitation-dependence) and surface molecular state emission (generally being excitation-independent) are commonly evolved to explain the mechanism [54]. Moreover, Pan et al have proposed that the solvent formamide or its decomposed products played an important role in the preparation of these C-dots, because other solvents like water, and ethanol do not perform [55]. Through complex separation methods, Holá et al and Ding et al have demonstrated the red-shift of C-dots was ascribed to graphitic nitrogen, and oxygen species in the carbon dots surface structure, separately [56,57]. Recently, Zhang et al have introduced C-dots simultaneously exhibiting high-color-purity (FWHM ∼ 24 nm) long wavelength one-photon fluorescence emission at 620 nm and NIR-induced two-photon fluorescence emission at 630 and 680 nm. They showed that the fluorophore products of precursor conversion were viable determinants of the desired luminescence properties of C-dots [58]. In this study, the emission spectrum of the prepared C-dots for red fluorescence is excitation-independent (Fig. S1B), so the mechanism of red fluorescence is likely to attribute the surface molecular state emission. However, due to the complexities in compositions, surface functionalities, and the crystal structures of different C-dots, further systematic analysis is needed to clarify the photoluminescence mechanism of C-dots.

Although C-dots have shown good therapeutic efficacy in animal models of DSS in colitis animal models, their true clinical effects in patients remain to be investigated. Meanwhile, C-dots have fluorescent properties but lack targeting and have limited enzymatic activity. In our future work, we will remain committed to developing nanozymes with lower cost and higher enzymatic activity for IBD treatment to validate their potential in clinical applications.

In summary, we utilized the synthesized C-dots nanozymes to alleviate intestinal inflammation through oral administration. The C-dots showed more accumulation in the colon than that of intravenous administration. In the DSS-induced colitis mice, the C-dots exerted excellent anti-inflammatory effects through scavenging ROS, which was superior to 5-ASA, the clinical first-line therapeutic drug. Furthermore, we found that the C-dots could regulate the abundance and diversity of intestinal flora using 16S RNA sequencing. Collectively, by remodeling redox and flora homeostasis, oral C-dots would provide a new avenue for UC therapeutics.
